results.possibleIdentity: The name "Tang lohan" (or "Tang Iohan") is likely a mishearing or phonetic transcription of the artist Jean Tinguely (often pronounced "Tang-ly") and his frequent collaborator and founder of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), Johan Wilhelm "Billy" Klüver. Jean Tinguely (1925–1991) was a Swiss sculptor best known for his kinetic art and self-destructing machines. His landmark 1960 work, "Homage to New York," a massive kinetic sculpture that destroyed itself in the garden of the Museum of Modern Art, was created with the technical assistance of Billy Klüver (Johan Klüver). This partnership was a foundational moment for E.A.T., which was formally established in 1966 by Klüver, Robert Rauschenberg, Robert Whitman, and Fred Waldhauer. Tinguely and Klüver's collaboration in the late 1960s and early 1970s exemplifies the organization's mission to bridge the gap between artists and engineers. The phonetic similarity between "Tinguely-Johan" and "Tang lohan" is documented in contemporary transcripts where Tinguely is sometimes mistakenly transcribed as "Tang Li."

The name may be a conflation of the Tang Teaching Museum and Art Gallery at Skidmore College and the architect Dirk Lohan. Dirk Lohan (born 1938) is a prominent modernist architect and the grandson of Ludwig Mies van der Rohe. He designed the building for the Mary and Leigh Block Museum of Art at Northwestern University, which has hosted significant exhibitions related to Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), such as "A Feast of Astonishments" (2015). The Tang Teaching Museum is also a major repository for artworks and archives associated with E.A.T. figures like Robert Rauschenberg. A user might associate the names "Tang" (the institution) and "Lohan" (the architect) in the context of historical research into E.A.T. projects and exhibitions from the late 1960s and early 1970s.
